title: Chicken and rice casserole
subtitle: Chicken thighs baked on top of creamy, herb-seasoned rice
excerpt: Bone-in chicken thighs laid skin-side up over long-grain rice, chicken stock, and cream, then baked covered for 45 minutes and uncovered for 15 more until the chicken is golden, the rice is cooked, and the liquid has been absorbed into a creamy, savoury base. One pan in the oven, one wash-up.
sourceNotes: Chicken and rice casseroles appear in mid-twentieth-century American domestic cooking, associated with the one-dish baking tradition that flourished with the proliferation of home ovens from the 1940s onward. The basic formula of protein and starch baked together in a creamy sauce is documented in American home economics publications and general cookbooks from the mid-twentieth century.

content.text: The rice cooks entirely in the oven, absorbing the stock and cream from below while the chicken colours above. The key is the liquid ratio: 300 ml of stock and 100 ml of cream to 200 g of long-grain rice gives rice that is cooked through without being wet or gluey. The dish must be covered tightly for the first 45 minutes to trap the steam; foil works if the dish has no lid. Removing the cover for the last 15 minutes crisps the chicken skin.

Season the chicken skin well before it goes on top of the rice; once the dish is assembled you cannot season the chicken without disturbing the rice layer. A little garlic powder and paprika on the skin adds colour and flavour without requiring any additional work. The dish should look golden and slightly bubbling at the edges when the foil comes off.

Heat the oven to 190°C fan. In the baking dish, stir together the {{long-grain-rice}} of rice, {{onion}}, {{garlic}} of crushed garlic, {{thyme}}, and a pinch of salt. Pour over the {{stock-chicken}} of chicken stock and {{double-cream}} of double cream and stir gently to distribute. Pat the {{chicken-thigh}} chicken thighs dry with kitchen paper and season on all sides with salt, pepper, {{paprika-sweet}} of paprika, and {{garlic-powder}} of garlic powder. Lay them skin-side up on top of the rice mixture. Cover the dish tightly with a lid or foil. Bake for 45 minutes.
Remove the foil or lid and return to the oven for a further 15 minutes until the chicken skin is golden and crisp and the rice has absorbed all the liquid. Rest for 5 minutes before serving.

items.fix: Seal the foil firmly around the rim; if the rice is still underdone after 45 minutes, add 50 ml of water and return, covered, for a further 10 minutes
items.cause: Too little liquid, or dish not sealed tightly enough and steam escaped
items.symptom: Rice is still crunchy after baking

items.fix: Stick to the stated liquid ratio; if using a very deep dish, the steam takes longer to work through and the rice may over-absorb
Ensure the foil is fully removed for the last 15 minutes; if the skin is still pale after 15 minutes, give it another 5 to 10 minutes uncovered
items.cause: Dish not uncovered for long enough, or oven temperature too low
items.symptom: Chicken skin is pale and rubbery

items.fix: Add an extra 50 ml of stock at assembly; different rice varieties absorb different amounts
items.cause: Liquid absorbed too quickly during the covered phase
items.symptom: Dish is dry and sticking to the baking dish

content.text: The one-dish casserole is a mid-twentieth-century American kitchen staple. It came out of an era that valued cheap, hands-off cooking and a reliable oven timer. Chicken and rice became a weeknight standard: cheap, easy once assembled, and a dish most people at the table will eat. There is no single original recipe, just a formula every home cook lands on for herself: protein, starch, liquid, seasoning, oven. The formula works, which is why it has stayed in kitchens for decades.
